questions.q: What is a View Controller in iOS development?
options: An object that manages a single screen's user interface and responds to user input
A class that handles network requests from a server
A database manager for storing app data locally
A configuration file that defines app capabilities
questions.explanation: A View Controller is the core component of iOS app structure. It owns a view and manages the logic for that screen. Network requests use URLSession or third-party libraries, data storage uses CoreData or UserDefaults, and capabilities are defined in info.plist.

questions.q: What is the role of a Storyboard in iOS development?
options: A visual tool to design UI layouts and define navigation between View Controllers
A text-based configuration file for app settings
A framework for managing data persistence
A library of reusable Swift functions
questions.explanation: Storyboards provide a graphical interface to design screens and define segues (transitions) between View Controllers. They work alongside code or as an alternative to programmatic UI construction. A misconception is that Storyboards are mandatory in modern development.

questions.q: Which lifecycle method is called when a View Controller is first created in memory?
options: viewDidLoad()
viewWillAppear()
init()
viewDidAppear()
questions.explanation: viewDidLoad() is called once after the View Controller's view is loaded into memory. It is the ideal place to configure views or load initial data. init() initializes the object itself but before the view exists, viewWillAppear() is called before visibility, and viewDidAppear() is called after.

questions.q: How does the View Controller lifecycle differ between viewWillAppear() and viewDidAppear()?
options: viewWillAppear() is called before the view becomes visible; viewDidAppear() is called after it is fully visible and interactive
viewWillAppear() initializes the view; viewDidAppear() destroys it
viewWillAppear() is called once during initialization; viewDidAppear() is called on every return
viewDidAppear() is faster than viewWillAppear()
questions.explanation: viewWillAppear() fires before the view is shown (good for final setup), while viewDidAppear() fires after it is displayed (good for starting animations or timers). Both are called every time the view appears. A misconception is that viewDidAppear() is called before viewWillAppear().

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Segue in iOS Storyboards?
options: To define a navigation transition between two View Controllers
To connect a UI element to a variable in code
To manage the app's data model
To configure network connections
questions.explanation: A Segue represents a visual transition from one View Controller to another. It can be triggered by a button tap or programmatically. IBOutlets connect UI elements to code, data models are managed separately, and networking is handled through URLSession or libraries.

questions.q: In Swift, what is the difference between a class and a struct?
options: Classes are reference types; structs are value types and do not support inheritance
Classes are value types; structs are reference types
Structs cannot contain methods; classes can
Classes are used for UI; structs are used only for data
questions.explanation: Classes use reference semantics and support inheritance; structs are value types and do not support inheritance. A misconception is that structs cannot have methods. Modern Swift design favors structs by default for safety.

questions.q: What is the purpose of @IBOutlet in iOS development?
options: To create a reference from code to a UI element defined in Storyboard or XIB
To define a public variable accessible from other classes
To trigger an action when a UI event occurs
To configure the appearance of a view in code
questions.explanation: @IBOutlet creates a connection between a UI element in the Interface Builder and a property in your View Controller. @IBAction connects UI events to methods, public modifier makes variables accessible externally, and appearance is configured through properties.

questions.q: How does iOS handle memory management for objects created in ARC (Automatic Reference Counting)?
options: ARC automatically increases and decreases retain counts; when count reaches zero, the object is deallocated
Objects are deallocated when the view controller is dismissed, regardless of retain counts
Developers must manually call release() to deallocate objects
ARC is only used for UI objects; other objects require manual memory management
questions.explanation: ARC tracks strong references and automatically deallocates objects when no strong references exist. This eliminates the need for manual release() calls. A misconception is that ARC only applies to UI elements. Retain cycles can prevent deallocation, requiring weak references.

questions.q: What is a weak reference in Swift, and when should it be used?
options: A reference that does not increase the retain count and is set to nil when the object is deallocated; used to break retain cycles
A reference that creates a copy of an object to avoid mutation
A reference type that behaves like a struct
A reference that is only valid during the current function scope
questions.explanation: Weak references prevent retain cycles where A holds a strong reference to B, and B holds a strong reference to A. The weak reference is automatically set to nil when the referenced object is deallocated. A misconception is that weak references are always necessary.

questions.q: What is the difference between a delegate and a closure for handling callbacks in Swift?
options: Delegates use a protocol-based pattern with a separate object; closures capture values directly and are more flexible for simple callbacks
Delegates are always faster than closures
Closures are only used in async operations; delegates are for synchronous operations
Both are identical in performance and functionality
questions.explanation: Delegates follow the delegate pattern where a separate object conforms to a protocol and implements callback methods; closures are blocks of code that can be assigned to variables. Delegates are more formal and reusable; closures are concise for one-off callbacks.
